records.ety: [OE. dwergh, dwerf, dwarf, AS. dweorg, dweorh; akin to D. dwerg, MHG. twerc, G. zwerg, Icel. dvergr, Sw. & Dan. dverg; of unknown origin.]

defs: An animal or plant which is much below the ordinary size of its species or kind.

A diminutive human being, small in stature due to a pathological condition which causes a distortion of the proportions of body parts to each other, such as the limbs, torso, and head. A person of unusually small height who has normal body proportions is usually called a midget.
notes: ☞ During the Middle Ages dwarfs as well as fools shared the favor of courts and the nobility.

defs: A small, usually misshapen person, typically a man, who may have magical powers; mythical dwarves were often depicted as living underground in caves.
notes: Dwarf is used adjectively in reference to anything much below the usual or normal size; as, a dwarf pear tree; dwarf honeysuckle.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Dwarfed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Dwarfing.]

defs: To hinder from growing to the natural size; to make or keep small; to stunt.
quotes: Even the most common moral ideas and affections . . . would be stunted and dwarfed, if cut off from a spiritual background.

defs: To become small; to diminish in size.
quotes: Strange power of the world that, the moment we enter it, our great conceptions dwarf.
